1:
"Paul, called to be an apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God, and Sosthenes our brother," The witnesses note Paul's apostolic greeting. On "called to be an apostle... through the will of God," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens by asserting his apostleship — called (divinely summoned, not self-appointed) to be an apostle of Christ, and this "through the will of God," his commission resting on God's sovereign appointment, not human choice.

On "and Sosthenes our brother," the witnesses note the companion. Barnes notes Paul joins Sosthenes (likely the former ruler of the Corinthian synagogue, Acts 18:17) with himself in the greeting — a brother known to the Corinthians, though not co-author of the letter's authority.

The eastern Chrysostom and latin Tertullian note the weight: Paul stresses his divine calling at the outset because the Corinthians, given to faction, needed reminding of his God-given authority. The verse displays Paul's apostolic greeting grounded in the will of God.

The witnesses agree: Paul opens by asserting his apostleship — called to be an apostle of Christ through the will of God, his commission resting on God's sovereign appointment, not human choice (Barnes, Robertson) — joining Sosthenes (likely the former synagogue ruler, Acts 18:17) as a brother known to the Corinthians (Barnes). Chrysostom and Tertullian note Paul stresses his divine calling because the faction-prone Corinthians needed reminding of his authority. The verse displays Paul's apostolic greeting. The verse marks the office: called to be an apostle. The verse displays the ground: the will of God. The verse displays the companion: Sosthenes. The verse displays the purpose: asserting authority. The greeting displays the divine ground of Paul's apostleship. The verse displays the calling: divine, not self-made. The verse displays the will: of God. The verse displays the brother: Sosthenes. Paul's apostolic greeting, grounded in the will of God, displays the divine authority with which he addresses the divided Corinthians: called to be an apostle not by his own choosing but by God's sovereign will, he writes with the weight of a God-given commission. The verse opens the epistle by establishing Paul's authority, and addresses the church and its sanctified standing (v.2).
2:
"Unto the church of God which is at Corinth, to them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, with all that in every place call upon the name of Jesus Christ our Lord, both theirs and ours:" The witnesses note the address to the sanctified church. On "the church of God at Corinth," the grammatical voices note the recipients. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses the church of God — not of Paul or any party — at Corinth, a pointed reminder against their factions.

On "sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints," the witnesses note their standing. Barnes and Calvin note the Corinthians are "sanctified in Christ" (set apart, consecrated to God in him) and "called to be saints" (summoned to holiness) — their high calling, despite their faults.

On "with all that in every place call upon the name of Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the breadth: the letter belongs to all who call on Christ's name everywhere — and "calling on the name of Jesus" implies his deity, prayer being offered to him. The verse displays the address to the sanctified church.

The witnesses agree: Paul addresses "the church of God" (not of any party) at Corinth, a reminder against their factions (Barnes, Robertson) — those "sanctified in Christ Jesus" and "called to be saints," their high calling to holiness despite their faults (Barnes, Calvin) — together with all who call on Christ's name everywhere, the "calling on the name of Jesus" implying his deity (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the address to the church. The verse marks the recipients: the church of God. The verse displays the standing: sanctified, called to be saints. The verse displays the breadth: all that call on Christ. The verse displays the deity: prayer to Christ. The address displays the sanctified standing of the called. The verse displays the church: of God. The verse displays the sanctification: in Christ. The verse displays the calling: to be saints. The address to the sanctified church displays the high standing of the called: the Corinthians, for all their divisions, are the church of God, sanctified in Christ and summoned to holiness, joined to all who call on the name of Jesus everywhere. The verse displays their consecrated standing as the ground of the appeal to come, and pronounces grace and peace (v.3).
3:
"Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the apostolic benediction of grace and peace. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's customary greeting: grace (God's free favour) and peace (the reconciliation and tranquillity that flow from it) — the sum of gospel blessing.

On "from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the joint source. Barnes notes grace and peace come alike from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the two named together as one source of blessing, a mark of Christ's divine dignity.

The Reformed Calvin notes the order: grace precedes peace, for God's free favour is the fountain from which all peace flows; the blessing wholly of grace. The verse displays the benediction of grace and peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's customary greeting — grace (God's free favour) and peace (the reconciliation that flows from it), the sum of gospel blessing (Barnes, Robertson) — coming alike from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, the two named together as one source, a mark of Christ's divine dignity (Barnes). Calvin notes grace precedes peace, God's free favour the fountain of all peace. The verse displays the benediction. The verse marks the gifts: grace and peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Christ. The verse displays the dignity: Christ joined with the Father. The verse displays the order: grace then peace. The benediction displays grace and peace from God and Christ. The verse displays the grace: free favour. The verse displays the peace: its fruit. The verse displays the joint source: Father and Son. The apostolic benediction of grace and peace displays the sum of gospel blessing, flowing alike from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ: grace, the free favour of God, and peace, its fruit, bestowed by the Father and the Son together. The verse displays the gracious source of every blessing, and leads to Paul's thanksgiving for the Corinthians (v.4). That the same blessing is traced alike to the Father and to the Son, named together as one fountain, is no small testimony to the dignity of Christ, who shares with the Father the bestowal of all that the soul most needs.
4:
"I thank my God always on your behalf, for the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ;" The witnesses note Paul's thanksgiving for the grace given the Corinthians. On "I thank my God always on your behalf," the grammatical voices note the gratitude. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, before any rebuke, begins with thanksgiving — he constantly gives thanks to God on their behalf, a gracious opening even to a faulty church.

On "for the grace of God given you by Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes and Chrysostom note the cause of thanks: the grace (the spiritual gifts and favour) bestowed on them by (through) Christ — all their endowments owing to God's grace, not their merit.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the tact: Paul acknowledges what is good in them before correcting their faults, and ascribes all their gifts to grace, gently undercutting their pride. The verse displays Paul's thanksgiving for the grace given them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, before any rebuke, begins with thanksgiving — constantly giving thanks on their behalf, a gracious opening even to a faulty church (Barnes, Robertson) — for the grace (the gifts and favour) bestowed on them through Christ, all their endowments owing to grace not merit (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Calvin note Paul acknowledges what is good before correcting, ascribing all to grace and undercutting their pride. The verse displays Paul's thanksgiving. The verse marks the gratitude: I thank my God. The verse displays the ground: the grace given you. The verse displays the channel: by Jesus Christ. The verse displays the tact: praise before rebuke. The thanksgiving displays the grace freely given them. The verse displays the thanks: always. The verse displays the grace: bestowed. The verse displays the source: Christ. Paul's thanksgiving for the grace given the Corinthians displays his gracious approach to a faulty church: before correcting their divisions, he gives thanks for the gifts God had freely bestowed on them through Christ, ascribing all their endowments to grace. The verse displays the apostle's tactful gratitude, and specifies the riches of their endowment (vv.5-7).
5:
"That in every thing ye are enriched by him, in all utterance, and in all knowledge;" The brief verse notes the Corinthians enriched in utterance and knowledge. The grammatical voices note the endowment. Barnes and Robertson note that the Corinthians were enriched (made abundant) by Christ in everything — particularly in "all utterance" (the gift of speech, expression) and "all knowledge" (understanding of divine truth).

On "enriched by him," the witnesses note the source. Barnes notes their abundance of gifts came from Christ — the wealth of their endowment was his gift, not their attainment.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the Corinthian character: this church was notably rich in spiritual gifts (utterance and knowledge), though, as the epistle shows, lacking in love and order. The verse displays the Corinthians enriched in utterance and knowledge.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Corinthians were enriched (made abundant) by Christ in everything — particularly in "all utterance" (the gift of speech) and "all knowledge" (understanding of divine truth, Barnes, Robertson) — their abundance of gifts coming from Christ, not their own attainment (Barnes). Wesley notes this church was notably rich in gifts, though lacking in love and order. The verse displays the Corinthians enriched. The verse marks the endowment: enriched in everything. The verse displays the gifts: utterance and knowledge. The verse displays the source: by him. The verse displays the character: rich in gifts. The enrichment displays the abundant gifts bestowed on them. The verse displays the wealth: of endowment. The verse displays the utterance: the gift of speech. The verse displays the knowledge: of the truth. The Corinthians enriched in utterance and knowledge display the abundant gifts Christ had bestowed on them: they were made rich in everything, especially in eloquence and the understanding of divine truth. The verse displays the wealth of their endowment, all owing to Christ, and confirms it as the fruit of the gospel's witness (v.6). This wealth of speech and knowledge, freely conferred and not earned, was meant to serve the upbuilding of the whole body; yet the Corinthians, as the epistle will show, turned these very gifts into occasions of pride and faction, forgetting the love without which all gifts are nothing.
6:
"Even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you:" The brief verse notes the testimony of Christ confirmed among them. The grammatical voices note the confirmation. Barnes and Robertson note "the testimony of Christ" (the gospel witness concerning Christ, which Paul preached) was confirmed (established, made firm) in them — by their reception of it and by the gifts that accompanied it.

On "confirmed in you," the witnesses note the means. Barnes notes the gospel was confirmed among them both by their faith and by the spiritual gifts (utterance, knowledge) which attested its truth and power.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the enrichment of the Corinthians (v.5) was the seal and confirmation of the gospel testimony among them — their gifts proving the genuineness of the word they received. The verse displays the testimony of Christ confirmed among them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "the testimony of Christ" (the gospel witness concerning Christ which Paul preached) was confirmed (established, made firm) in them by their reception of it and the gifts that accompanied it (Barnes, Robertson); the gospel confirmed both by their faith and by the gifts attesting its power (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the enrichment of the Corinthians was the seal of the gospel testimony among them. The verse displays the testimony confirmed. The verse marks the witness: the testimony of Christ. The verse displays the establishing: confirmed in you. The verse displays the means: faith and gifts. The verse displays the seal: their endowment. The confirmation displays the gospel established among them. The verse displays the testimony: of Christ. The verse displays the confirming: in them. The verse displays the proof: the gifts. The testimony of Christ confirmed among them displays the gospel established in the Corinthians: the witness concerning Christ which Paul preached was made firm among them by their faith and by the spiritual gifts that attested its truth. The verse displays the seal set on the gospel by their endowment, and concludes that they lack no gift (v.7). The gifts they had received were thus no accident, but the visible seal of a gospel that had taken firm root among them, attesting both to the truth of the message and to the faithfulness of the God who had called them.
7:
"So that ye come behind in no gift; waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the Corinthians lacking no gift while awaiting Christ's coming. On "ye come behind in no gift," the grammatical voices note the abundance. Barnes and Robertson note the Corinthians fell short in no spiritual gift (charisma) — richly endowed, behind no other church in the gifts of the Spirit.

On "waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the posture. Barnes and Cyril note the believers were "waiting for" (eagerly expecting) the revelation (coming) of Christ — their gifts held in an attitude of expectant hope toward his return.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the orientation: the right use of present gifts is joined to the expectation of Christ's appearing; the Corinthians, for all their endowment, were to live looking for his coming. The verse displays the Corinthians lacking no gift while awaiting Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Corinthians fell short in no spiritual gift — richly endowed, behind no other church in the gifts of the Spirit (Barnes, Robertson) — while "waiting for" (eagerly expecting) the coming of Christ, their gifts held in expectant hope toward his return (Barnes, Cyril). Calvin and Cyril note the right use of present gifts is joined to the expectation of Christ's appearing. The verse displays the Corinthians lacking no gift. The verse marks the abundance: behind in no gift. The verse displays the posture: waiting for Christ. The verse displays the hope: expectant. The verse displays the orientation: toward his coming. The abundance displays their full endowment held in expectant hope. The verse displays the gifts: complete. The verse displays the waiting: for Christ. The verse displays the expectation: his coming. The Corinthians lacking no gift while awaiting Christ's coming display their full endowment held in expectant hope: behind no church in the gifts of the Spirit, they were to use them while looking for the revelation of the Lord Jesus. The verse displays the union of present endowment and future expectation, and grounds their security in Christ's confirming grace (v.8).
8:
"Who shall also confirm you unto the end, that ye may be blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the assurance of being confirmed blameless to the end. On "who shall confirm you unto the end," the grammatical voices note the preservation. Barnes and Robertson note that God (or Christ) shall confirm (establish, strengthen) the believers unto the end — keeping them firm to the close of life, the perseverance grounded in God's faithfulness (v.9).

On "blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the goal. Barnes notes the end of this confirming: that they may be found blameless (free from accusation, acquitted) in the day of Christ — the day of judgment, when they shall stand unaccused.

The Reformed Calvin notes the assurance: this confirming is God's work, not man's — the saints' perseverance to the end and their blamelessness at the last rest on God's faithful keeping. The wesleyan-arminian reads it as the promise to those who continue. The verse displays the assurance of being confirmed blameless to the end.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God (or Christ) shall confirm (establish, strengthen) the believers unto the end, keeping them firm to the close of life (Barnes, Robertson), that they may be found blameless (free from accusation) in the day of Christ, the day of judgment when they shall stand unaccused (Barnes). Calvin notes this confirming is God's work, the saints' perseverance resting on his faithful keeping; the Arminian reads it as the promise to those who continue. The verse displays the assurance of being confirmed blameless. The verse marks the preservation: confirm you to the end. The verse displays the goal: blameless in the day of Christ. The verse displays the ground: God's faithfulness. The verse displays the day: of judgment. The assurance displays God's keeping of the saints to the end. The verse displays the confirming: to the end. The verse displays the blamelessness: at the last. The verse displays the day: of Christ. The assurance of being confirmed blameless to the end displays God's faithful keeping of his people: he who began the work will establish them firm to the close, that they may stand unaccused in the day of Christ. The verse displays the security of the believer grounded in God's faithfulness, which it next declares directly (v.9).
9:
"God is faithful, by whom ye were called unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ our Lord." The witnesses note God's faithfulness as the ground of the believer's calling and security. On "God is faithful," the grammatical voices note the foundation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds the assurance of v.8 in God's faithfulness — God is faithful (true to his promises, unchanging), and therefore will surely keep those he has called.

On "by whom ye were called," the witnesses note the calling. Barnes and Calvin note the believers were called (effectually summoned, by God's grace) — and since God who called them is faithful, he will not abandon his own work.

On "unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the end: the calling is into fellowship (communion, participation) with Christ — union with the Son, sharing in him and his benefits. The verse displays God's faithfulness as the ground of calling and security.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grounds the assurance of v.8 in God's faithfulness — God is faithful (true to his promises, unchanging) and will surely keep those he has called (Barnes, Robertson); the believers being effectually called by grace, and God who called them being faithful, he will not abandon his work (Barnes, Calvin) — the calling being into fellowship (communion) with Christ, union with the Son (Calvin, Cyril). The verse displays God's faithfulness. The verse marks the foundation: God is faithful. The verse displays the calling: by him. The verse displays the end: fellowship with Christ. The verse displays the security: he will keep his own. God's faithfulness displays the sure ground of the believer's security. The verse displays the faithfulness: of God. The verse displays the calling: effectual. The verse displays the fellowship: with the Son. God's faithfulness as the ground of the believer's calling and security displays the unshakable foundation of their hope: since God, who is faithful, has called them into fellowship with his Son, he will surely keep them to the end. The verse displays the divine faithfulness that secures the saints, and turns to the grievous divisions among them (v.10).
10:
"Now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you; but that ye be perfectly joined together in the same mind and in the same judgment." The witnesses note the plea for unity against divisions. On "I beseech you... that ye all speak the same thing," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul earnestly entreats them, by the name (authority) of Christ, to speak the same thing — to be of one accord in their profession, ending their party slogans.

On "no divisions among you," the witnesses note the evil addressed. Barnes notes "divisions" (schismata, rents, factions) were tearing the church; Paul pleads for their removal.

On "perfectly joined together in the same mind and judgment," the eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the goal: the believers are to be knit together (as a body, or a mended net) in the same mind (disposition) and judgment (opinion) — a complete restoration of harmony. The verse displays the plea for unity against divisions.

The witnesses agree: Paul earnestly entreats them, by the name (authority) of Christ, to speak the same thing — to be of one accord, ending their party slogans (Barnes, Robertson) — that there be no "divisions" (schismata, factions) tearing the church (Barnes), but that they be "perfectly joined together" (knit as a body or mended net) in the same mind and judgment, a complete restoration of harmony (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the plea for unity. The verse marks the appeal: speak the same thing. The verse displays the evil: divisions. The verse displays the goal: perfectly joined together. The verse displays the harmony: same mind and judgment. The plea displays the call to unity against faction. The verse displays the entreaty: by Christ's name. The verse displays the division: to be healed. The verse displays the union: restored. The plea for unity against divisions displays the great burden of the epistle's opening: Paul beseeches the Corinthians, by the name of Christ, to abandon their factions and be perfectly knit together in one mind and judgment. The verse displays the call to harmony that confronts their party spirit, and names the source of his information (v.11).
11:
"For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren, by them which are of the house of Chloe, that there are contentions among you." The brief verse notes the report of contentions from the house of Chloe. The grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul names his informants: members of the household of Chloe (a Corinthian woman, perhaps a merchant whose people travelled) had reported to him the contentions among the believers.

On "contentions among you," the witnesses note the evil. Barnes notes "contentions" (erides, strifes, quarrels) — the factions had erupted into open disputes; Paul does not deal in rumour but in a credible report.

The Reformed Calvin notes the candour: Paul openly names his source, neither concealing the trouble nor dealing in vague hearsay — a model of honest, loving rebuke. The verse displays the report of contentions from the house of Chloe.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul names his informants — members of the household of Chloe (a Corinthian woman whose people travelled) had reported the contentions among the believers (Barnes, Robertson); "contentions" (erides, strifes) marking that the factions had erupted into open disputes, Paul dealing not in rumour but a credible report (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul openly names his source, a model of honest, loving rebuke. The verse displays the report of contentions. The verse marks the source: the house of Chloe. The verse displays the evil: contentions. The verse displays the candour: named informants. The verse displays the credibility: a true report. The report displays the open ground of Paul's rebuke. The verse displays the informants: Chloe's house. The verse displays the strifes: among them. The verse displays the honesty: named source. The report of contentions from the house of Chloe displays the credible ground of Paul's rebuke: he had learned of their open strifes from trustworthy members of Chloe's household, and deals with the trouble candidly rather than on rumour. The verse displays the apostle's honest dealing, and specifies the form the factions took (v.12). By dealing thus openly, neither hiding the trouble nor magnifying it on uncertain rumour, the apostle shows the manner in which faults are rightly to be confronted in the church, with candour and with love rather than with whispered suspicion.
12:
"Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul; and I of Apollos; and I of Cephas; and I of Christ." The witnesses note the party slogans dividing the church. On the four slogans, the grammatical voices note the factions. Barnes and Robertson note the Corinthians had split into parties, each rallying under a name: "I am of Paul" (the founder), "of Apollos" (the eloquent teacher), "of Cephas" (Peter, perhaps the Jewish-leaning), and "of Christ" (a party even claiming Christ exclusively, perhaps with spiritual pride).

On the "of Christ" party, the witnesses note the irony. Barnes and Calvin note that even the party that boasted "I am of Christ" was at fault — making Christ a party-name, dividing rather than uniting; for Christ is not the head of a faction but of the whole.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the root: the factions arose from attaching themselves to human teachers (and even abusing Christ's name) rather than to Christ as the one head of all. The verse displays the party slogans dividing the church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Corinthians had split into parties, each rallying under a name — "of Paul" (the founder), "of Apollos" (the eloquent teacher), "of Cephas" (Peter), and "of Christ" (a party claiming Christ exclusively, perhaps with pride, Barnes, Robertson); even the "of Christ" party at fault, making Christ a party-name and dividing rather than uniting (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the factions arose from attaching to human teachers rather than to Christ the one head. The verse displays the party slogans. The verse marks the factions: Paul, Apollos, Cephas, Christ. The verse displays the irony: even the Christ-party at fault. The verse displays the root: attaching to men. The verse displays the head: Christ of all. The slogans display the divisive party spirit at Corinth. The verse displays the names: rallying-points. The verse displays the division: into factions. The verse displays the fault: even the Christ-party. The party slogans dividing the church display the divisive spirit at Corinth: the believers rallied under the names of Paul, Apollos, Cephas, and even Christ as a faction, attaching themselves to human teachers rather than owning Christ as the one head of all. The verse displays the concrete form of their divisions, and confronts the absurdity of dividing Christ (v.13).
13:
"Is Christ divided? was Paul crucified for you? or were ye baptized in the name of Paul?" The witnesses note the three questions exposing the folly of faction. On "Is Christ divided?" the grammatical voices note the absurdity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's first question: Christ is one and undivided; to split into parties is to rend the body of Christ, an absurdity — Christ cannot be parcelled out among factions.

On "was Paul crucified for you?" the witnesses note the second. Barnes notes Paul appeals to the cross: it was Christ, not Paul, who was crucified for them; their salvation rests on Christ's death, not any human teacher — so to be "of Paul" is folly.

On "were ye baptized in the name of Paul?" the Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the third: they were baptized into Christ's name, not Paul's — pledged to Christ, belonging to him alone. The verse displays the three questions exposing the folly of faction.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree Paul exposes the folly of faction by three questions: "Is Christ divided?" — Christ is one, and to split into parties rends his body, an absurdity (Barnes, Robertson); "was Paul crucified for you?" — it was Christ, not Paul, who died for them, so to be "of Paul" is folly (Barnes); and "were ye baptized in the name of Paul?" — they were baptized into Christ's name, pledged to him alone (Calvin, Cyril). The verse displays the three questions. The verse marks the first: Christ not divided. The verse displays the second: Paul not crucified for them. The verse displays the third: not baptized into Paul. The verse displays the point: Christ alone their Lord. The questions display the absurdity of the party spirit. The verse displays the unity: of Christ. The verse displays the cross: Christ's, not Paul's. The verse displays the baptism: into Christ. The three questions exposing the folly of faction display the absurdity of the Corinthian parties: Christ is undivided, Paul was not crucified for them, and they were baptized into Christ's name, not Paul's — so to rally under human names is to deny that Christ alone is their crucified, owning Lord. The verse displays the crushing logic against their divisions, and leads Paul to recall how few he baptized (vv.14-16).
14:
"I thank God that I baptized none of you, but Crispus and Gaius;" The witnesses note Paul's thankfulness that he baptized few. On "I thank God that I baptized none of you, but Crispus and Gaius," the grammatical voices note the recollection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, in the heat of the argument, recalls that he personally baptized very few at Corinth — only Crispus (the former synagogue ruler, Acts 18:8) and Gaius — so none could claim to be his party through his baptizing them.

On the thankfulness, the witnesses note the reason. Barnes notes Paul is thankful not that baptism is unimportant, but that, providentially, he had baptized so few that no one could pretend he sought to make disciples to himself.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the guard: Paul does not disparage baptism, but rejoices that the circumstance removed all ground for the faction that bore his name. The verse displays Paul's thankfulness that he baptized few.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls that he personally baptized very few at Corinth — only Crispus (the former synagogue ruler) and Gaius — so none could claim to be his party through his baptizing them (Barnes, Robertson); thankful not that baptism is unimportant, but that providentially he had baptized so few that none could pretend he sought disciples to himself (Barnes). Henry and Calvin note Paul does not disparage baptism but rejoices the circumstance removed ground for the faction bearing his name. The verse displays Paul's thankfulness. The verse marks the recollection: baptized none but Crispus and Gaius. The verse displays the reason: no party through baptism. The verse displays the guard: not disparaging baptism. The verse displays the providence: few baptized. The thankfulness displays the removal of ground for faction. The verse displays the few: Crispus and Gaius. The verse displays the relief: no party of Paul. The verse displays the care: baptism honoured. Paul's thankfulness that he baptized few displays his concern to give no ground for faction: having personally baptized only Crispus and Gaius, he rejoices that none could claim to be his party through baptism — not slighting the ordinance, but glad the circumstance silenced the slogan that bore his name. The verse displays the apostle's care to keep the focus on Christ, and continues the recollection (vv.15-16).
15:
"Lest any should say that I had baptized in mine own name." The brief verse notes Paul's concern to give no ground for personal allegiance. The grammatical voices note the reason. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states why he is glad he baptized few: lest anyone should say he had baptized in his own name — that is, to make disciples to himself, gathering a following around his own person.

On "in mine own name," the witnesses note the danger avoided. Barnes notes the danger was that baptism by Paul might be misused to claim a special attachment to him; he is glad no such pretext existed.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: ministers baptize into Christ's name, not their own; the people belong to Christ, not the one who baptizes them. The verse displays Paul's concern to give no ground for personal allegiance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul states why he is glad he baptized few — lest anyone should say he had baptized in his own name, making disciples to himself and gathering a following around his person (Barnes, Robertson); the danger being that baptism by Paul might be misused to claim a special attachment to him (Barnes). Calvin notes ministers baptize into Christ's name, not their own; the people belong to Christ. The verse displays Paul's concern. The verse marks the reason: lest any say. The verse displays the danger: baptized in his name. The verse displays the principle: into Christ's name. The verse displays the relief: no such pretext. The concern displays Paul's refusal of personal allegiance. The verse displays the avoidance: of a following. The verse displays the name: not Paul's. The verse displays the belonging: to Christ. Paul's concern to give no ground for personal allegiance displays his self-effacing care: he is glad he baptized few, lest any should claim he baptized in his own name to gather a following — for the baptized belong to Christ, into whose name alone they are baptized. The verse displays the apostle's refusal to be the head of a party, and adds one further recollection (v.16).
16:
"And I baptized also the household of Stephanas: besides, I know not whether I baptized any other." The brief verse notes Paul's added recollection of baptizing Stephanas's house. The grammatical voices note the correction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, recalling more exactly, adds that he also baptized the household of Stephanas (the firstfruits of Achaia, 16:15) — and beyond these, he does not remember baptizing any other.

On "the household of Stephanas," the witnesses note the family baptism. Barnes notes Paul baptized a whole household — a fact often cited in discussions of household baptism, though Paul's point here is simply the fewness of those he baptized.

The grammatical-historical note the candour: Paul's uncertainty ("I know not whether I baptized any other") shows his honest, unstudied memory — and confirms that baptizing was not his chief work. The verse displays Paul's added recollection of baptizing Stephanas's house.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, recalling more exactly, adds that he also baptized the household of Stephanas (the firstfruits of Achaia, 16:15), and beyond these does not remember baptizing any other (Barnes, Robertson); the household baptism a fact often cited in such discussions, though Paul's point is simply the fewness of those he baptized (Barnes). His uncertainty shows his honest memory and confirms baptizing was not his chief work. The verse displays Paul's added recollection. The verse marks the addition: the household of Stephanas. The verse displays the uncertainty: knows not any other. The verse displays the candour: honest memory. The verse displays the point: fewness of baptisms. The recollection displays Paul's exact and honest accounting. The verse displays the household: of Stephanas. The verse displays the limit: none other recalled. The verse displays the focus: not on baptizing. Paul's added recollection of baptizing Stephanas's house displays his exact and candid accounting: correcting himself, he names the one further household, and confesses he recalls no other — confirming that baptizing was not his chief work. The verse displays the apostle's honest memory and the subordination of baptizing to preaching, which he now states directly (v.17).
17:
"For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel: not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect." The witnesses note the priority of preaching over rhetorical wisdom. On "Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach," the grammatical voices note the commission. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's chief commission was to preach the gospel — not that baptism was forbidden or unimportant, but that preaching was his primary work, baptism often left to others.

On "not with wisdom of words," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes and Calvin note Paul preached not with "wisdom of words" (rhetorical eloquence, philosophical artifice, the polished oratory the Greeks prized) — deliberately renouncing such display.

On "lest the cross of Christ be made of none effect," the Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the reason: had Paul relied on eloquence, the cross would be emptied of its power — men crediting the preacher's skill, not the gospel's truth; the cross's power must shine without the props of human wisdom. The verse displays the priority of preaching over rhetorical wisdom.

The witnesses agree: Paul's chief commission was to preach the gospel — not that baptism was forbidden or unimportant, but that preaching was his primary work (Barnes, Robertson) — and to preach "not with wisdom of words" (rhetorical eloquence, the polished oratory the Greeks prized), deliberately renouncing such display (Barnes, Calvin); for had he relied on eloquence, the cross would be emptied of its power, men crediting the preacher's skill rather than the gospel's truth (Calvin, Henry). The verse displays the priority of preaching. The verse marks the commission: to preach, not baptize. The verse displays the manner: not wisdom of words. The verse displays the reason: lest the cross be emptied. The verse displays the power: of the cross alone. The priority displays the cross set above human eloquence. The verse displays the preaching: Paul's chief work. The verse displays the renunciation: of rhetoric. The verse displays the cross: kept in its power. The priority of preaching over rhetorical wisdom displays Paul's resolve that the cross retain its power: sent chiefly to preach, he renounced the wisdom of words lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its effect by men crediting the preacher's eloquence rather than the gospel itself. The verse displays the great theme now opened — the cross against worldly wisdom — and unfolds it (vv.18-25).
18:
"For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness; but unto us which are saved it is the power of God." The witnesses note the cross as foolishness to the perishing but God's power to the saved. On "the preaching of the cross is foolishness to them that perish," the grammatical voices note the world's verdict. Barnes and Robertson note that to those who are perishing (the unbelieving, on the road to ruin), the message of a crucified Saviour appears foolishness (absurd, contemptible) — they see no wisdom or power in it.

On "unto us which are saved it is the power of God," the witnesses note the believer's experience. Barnes and Chrysostom note that to those being saved, the same cross is "the power of God" — the very means by which God exerts his saving power; they experience its converting, transforming force.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the division: the same gospel divides mankind — folly to the perishing, power to the saved; the difference lies not in the message but in the hearers' state. The verse displays the cross as foolishness to the perishing but God's power to the saved.

The witnesses agree: to those perishing (the unbelieving, on the road to ruin), the message of a crucified Saviour appears foolishness, contemptible, void of wisdom or power (Barnes, Robertson); but to those being saved, the same cross is "the power of God" — the means by which God exerts his saving, transforming force (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Calvin note the same gospel divides mankind — folly to the perishing, power to the saved, the difference lying in the hearers' state. The verse displays the cross as folly and power. The verse marks the world's verdict: foolishness. The verse displays the believer's experience: the power of God. The verse displays the division: perishing and saved. The verse displays the cause: the hearers' state. The cross displays the dividing line between the perishing and the saved. The verse displays the folly: to the perishing. The verse displays the power: to the saved. The verse displays the gospel: one message, two verdicts. The cross as foolishness to the perishing but God's power to the saved displays the great dividing line the gospel draws: the same message of a crucified Christ is contemptible folly to those perishing and the very power of God to those being saved. The verse displays the radical division the cross creates, and confirms it from prophecy (v.19).
19:
"For it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent." The brief verse grounds the overthrow of human wisdom in prophecy. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah (29:14): God will destroy the wisdom of the wise and bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent — the divine purpose to overthrow the proud wisdom of men.

On the application, the witnesses note the point. Barnes notes Paul applies this to the gospel age: God, in the cross, sets aside and confounds the boasted wisdom of philosophers and sages, accomplishing salvation by means they despised.

The Reformed Calvin notes the sovereignty: it is God's deliberate design to abase human wisdom, that his own wisdom in the cross may shine; the prophecy foretold this overthrow. The verse displays the overthrow of human wisdom grounded in prophecy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14 — God will destroy the wisdom of the wise and bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent, the divine purpose to overthrow the proud wisdom of men (Barnes, Robertson); applied to the gospel age, God in the cross sets aside the boasted wisdom of philosophers, saving by means they despised (Barnes). Calvin notes it is God's deliberate design to abase human wisdom that his own wisdom in the cross may shine. The verse displays the overthrow of human wisdom. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the purpose: destroy the wisdom of the wise. The verse displays the application: the gospel age. The verse displays the sovereignty: God's design. The overthrow displays God's purpose to abase human wisdom. The verse displays the destroying: of the wise. The verse displays the nullifying: of the prudent. The verse displays the design: God's. The overthrow of human wisdom grounded in prophecy displays God's deliberate purpose: as Isaiah foretold, he will destroy the wisdom of the wise and confound the prudent, setting aside the boasted wisdom of men so that his own wisdom in the cross may prevail. The verse displays the prophetic warrant for the abasing of worldly wisdom, and presses the challenge to the sages of the age (v.20).
20:
"Where is the wise? where is the scribe? where is the disputer of this world? hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?" The witnesses note the challenge to the sages of the age. On "where is the wise? the scribe? the disputer?" the grammatical voices note the triumphant question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul issues a challenge: where now are the wise (the Greek philosopher), the scribe (the Jewish teacher), the disputer (the debater, sophist)? — they are confounded, unable to discover or accomplish what the gospel does.

On "hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?" the witnesses note the verdict. Barnes notes God has shown the world's wisdom to be folly — by saving men through the cross, which that wisdom could neither devise nor approve, he has exposed its emptiness.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: all the schools of human wisdom, Jewish and Greek alike, failed to bring men to God; God has made their vaunted wisdom foolish. The verse displays the challenge to the sages of the age.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul issues a triumphant challenge — where now are the wise (the Greek philosopher), the scribe (the Jewish teacher), the disputer (the debater)? — they are confounded, unable to accomplish what the gospel does (Barnes, Robertson); for God has shown the world's wisdom to be folly, saving men through the cross which that wisdom could neither devise nor approve (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note all the schools of human wisdom, Jewish and Greek, failed to bring men to God. The verse displays the challenge to the sages. The verse marks the question: where is the wise? The verse displays the confounding: they are silenced. The verse displays the verdict: their wisdom made foolish. The verse displays the failure: of all schools. The challenge displays the bankruptcy of worldly wisdom. The verse displays the wise: confounded. The verse displays the scribe and disputer: silenced. The verse displays the wisdom: made foolish. The challenge to the sages of the age — where is the wise, the scribe, the disputer? — displays the bankruptcy of worldly wisdom: God has made foolish the wisdom of the world, confounding philosopher and scribe alike, by accomplishing through the cross what all their schools could never devise. The verse displays the silencing of human wisdom before the gospel, and explains why God chose this way (v.21).
21:
"For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe." The witnesses note God's choice to save by the foolishness of preaching. On "in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God," the grammatical voices note the failure. Barnes and Robertson note that, in God's wise arrangement, the world (with all its philosophy) failed by its own wisdom to know God — human reason, left to itself, could not find or save.

On "it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe," the witnesses note the divine means. Barnes and Calvin note God chose to save through "the foolishness of preaching" (the message men deemed foolish — the cross) — not the act of preaching but the thing preached; and to save "them that believe," faith the appointed channel.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the design: God deliberately bypassed human wisdom, that salvation might be of grace through faith, and the glory wholly his. The verse displays God's choice to save by the foolishness of preaching.

The witnesses agree: in God's wise arrangement, the world (with all its philosophy) failed by its own wisdom to know God — human reason, left to itself, could not find or save (Barnes, Robertson); so God chose to save through "the foolishness of preaching" (the message men deemed foolish, the cross — not the act but the thing preached) and to save "them that believe," faith the appointed channel (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note God deliberately bypassed human wisdom, that salvation might be of grace through faith. The verse displays God's choice to save by preaching. The verse marks the failure: the world knew not God. The verse displays the means: the foolishness of preaching. The verse displays the channel: them that believe. The verse displays the design: salvation by grace. The choice displays God saving by the despised message. The verse displays the wisdom: God's arrangement. The verse displays the folly: of preaching. The verse displays the faith: the channel. God's choice to save by the foolishness of preaching displays his deliberate design: since the world by its own wisdom failed to know God, he was pleased to save believers through the message men counted foolish — the cross — so that salvation might be of grace through faith, and the glory his alone. The verse displays the divine wisdom in the despised gospel, and contrasts the demands of Jew and Greek (vv.22-24).
22:
"For the Jews require a sign, and the Greeks seek after wisdom:" The brief verse notes the differing demands of Jews and Greeks. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the two characteristic demands: the Jews require a sign (a miracle, a display of power, the proof they sought from any claimant), and the Greeks seek after wisdom (philosophical depth, rhetorical and intellectual brilliance).

On the two attitudes, the witnesses note the obstacles. Barnes notes each nation, by its characteristic demand, was predisposed to reject the cross — the Jews for its weakness (no triumphant sign), the Greeks for its simplicity (no philosophical system).

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: both Jew and Greek brought their own standard to the gospel, and by it stumbled at the cross — each preferring their own demand to God's appointed way. The verse displays the differing demands of Jews and Greeks.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the two characteristic demands — the Jews require a sign (a miracle, a display of power) and the Greeks seek after wisdom (philosophical depth, intellectual brilliance, Barnes, Robertson); each nation, by its demand, predisposed to reject the cross — the Jews for its weakness, the Greeks for its simplicity (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note both brought their own standard to the gospel and stumbled at the cross. The verse displays the differing demands. The verse marks the Jews: require a sign. The verse displays the Greeks: seek wisdom. The verse displays the obstacle: each predisposed to reject. The verse displays the standard: their own demand. The demands display the obstacles Jew and Greek brought to the cross. The verse displays the sign: the Jewish demand. The verse displays the wisdom: the Greek demand. The verse displays the stumbling: at the cross. The differing demands of Jews and Greeks display the obstacles each brought to the gospel: the Jews requiring a sign of power, the Greeks seeking philosophical wisdom — both predisposed by their own standard to stumble at a crucified Saviour. The verse displays the two great barriers to the cross, against which Paul sets the message he preached (v.23).
23:
"But we preach Christ crucified, unto the Jews a stumblingblock, and unto the Greeks foolishness;" The witnesses note Christ crucified, a stumblingblock and foolishness. On "we preach Christ crucified," the grammatical voices note the message. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's central proclamation: Christ, and him crucified — not a triumphant sign for the Jews nor a philosophy for the Greeks, but a crucified Messiah, the very thing both rejected.

On "unto the Jews a stumblingblock," the witnesses note the Jewish offence. Barnes notes a crucified Messiah was a stumbling-block (skandalon) to the Jews — they expected a conquering deliverer, and a crucified one seemed cursed, the opposite of their hope.

On "unto the Greeks foolishness," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the Greek scorn: to the Greeks, a crucified God was absurd, foolishness — beneath the dignity of their philosophy. The verse displays Christ crucified, a stumblingblock and foolishness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's central proclamation is Christ crucified — not a sign for the Jews nor a philosophy for the Greeks, but a crucified Messiah, the very thing both rejected (Barnes, Robertson); a stumbling-block (skandalon) to the Jews, who expected a conquering deliverer and saw a crucified one as cursed (Barnes); and foolishness to the Greeks, to whom a crucified God was absurd, beneath their philosophy (Cyril). The verse displays Christ crucified. The verse marks the message: Christ crucified. The verse displays the Jewish offence: a stumblingblock. The verse displays the Greek scorn: foolishness. The verse displays the rejection: by both. Christ crucified displays the message that offended both Jew and Greek. The verse displays the preaching: the crucified Christ. The verse displays the stumbling: of the Jews. The verse displays the folly: to the Greeks. Christ crucified, a stumblingblock to the Jews and foolishness to the Greeks, displays the message that offended the standards of both: Paul preached a crucified Messiah — a scandal to the Jews who sought a conqueror, and absurdity to the Greeks who sought wisdom — refusing to soften the cross to suit either. The verse displays the offence of the cross to the natural mind, set against its power to the called (v.24).
24:
"But unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God." The witnesses note Christ the power and wisdom of God to the called. On "unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks," the grammatical voices note the recipients. Barnes and Robertson note that to those effectually called (by God's grace, whether Jew or Greek), the same crucified Christ appears not as scandal or folly but as the power and wisdom of God.

On "Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God," the witnesses note the title. Barnes and Calvin note Christ is to the called the power of God (the means of their salvation, where the Jews sought a sign) and the wisdom of God (the embodiment of God's saving wisdom, where the Greeks sought wisdom) — meeting in the cross both demands in a higher way.

The Reformed Calvin notes the grace: it is the calling (God's effectual grace) that opens the eyes to see in the cross what the unbelieving cannot — power and wisdom. The verse displays Christ the power and wisdom of God to the called.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: to those effectually called (by grace, whether Jew or Greek), the same crucified Christ appears not as scandal or folly but as the power and wisdom of God (Barnes, Robertson) — the power of God (their salvation, where the Jews sought a sign) and the wisdom of God (God's saving wisdom embodied, where the Greeks sought wisdom), meeting both demands in a higher way (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes it is the calling that opens the eyes to see this. The verse displays Christ the power and wisdom. The verse marks the recipients: the called. The verse displays the power: of God. The verse displays the wisdom: of God. The verse displays the grace: the calling opens the eyes. Christ the power and wisdom displays the cross transformed for the called. The verse displays the called: Jew and Greek. The verse displays the power: meeting the sign. The verse displays the wisdom: meeting the demand. Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God to the called displays how the cross, scandal and folly to the unbelieving, becomes to those effectually called the very power and wisdom of God: in the crucified Christ they find both the saving power the Jews sought in signs and the wisdom the Greeks sought in philosophy. The verse displays the transforming effect of God's calling, and draws the paradox of divine strength in weakness (v.25).
25:
"Because the foolishness of God is wiser than men; and the weakness of God is stronger than men." The witnesses note the paradox of God's foolishness and weakness surpassing men. On "the foolishness of God is wiser than men," the grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note that what men deem foolishness in God (the cross, the gospel scheme) is in truth wiser than the highest human wisdom — God's "folly" outstrips man's wisdom.

On "the weakness of God is stronger than men," the witnesses note the parallel. Barnes notes what men deem weakness in God (a crucified, suffering Saviour) is stronger than all human strength — accomplishing what no human power could.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: God's seeming folly and weakness in the cross infinitely surpass the wisdom and might of men; the contrast is between God at his "weakest" (so men judge) and man at his strongest. The verse displays the paradox of God's foolishness and weakness surpassing men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: what men deem foolishness in God (the cross, the gospel scheme) is in truth wiser than the highest human wisdom (Barnes, Robertson), and what men deem weakness in God (a crucified, suffering Saviour) is stronger than all human strength, accomplishing what no human power could (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the contrast is between God at his seeming weakest and man at his strongest — God's "folly" and "weakness" infinitely surpassing both. The verse displays the paradox. The verse marks the foolishness: wiser than men. The verse displays the weakness: stronger than men. The verse displays the surpassing: God beyond man. The verse displays the contrast: divine and human. The paradox displays God's surpassing wisdom and power in the cross. The verse displays the "folly": wiser. The verse displays the "weakness": stronger. The verse displays the supremacy: of God. The paradox of God's foolishness and weakness surpassing men displays the supremacy of the cross: what men count folly and weakness in God — a crucified Saviour — is wiser and stronger than the highest wisdom and might of men, accomplishing salvation beyond all human power. The verse displays the divine glory hidden in the seeming weakness of the cross, and illustrates it in the lowly Corinthian believers (vv.26-29).
26:
"For ye see your calling, brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called:" The witnesses note that not many wise, mighty, or noble are called. On "ye see your calling," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul bids the Corinthians look at themselves — at the kind of people God had called — as evidence of the principle just stated.

On "not many wise, mighty, noble," the witnesses note the lowly composition. Barnes and Chrysostom note that among the called were not many of the wise (the learned), the mighty (the powerful), or the noble (the high-born) — the church was largely composed of the humble and lowly.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the design: God deliberately passed by the great of the world, choosing the lowly, that salvation might manifestly be of grace, not of human advantage. The verse displays that not many wise, mighty, or noble are called.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul bids the Corinthians look at their own calling — the kind of people God had called — as evidence of the principle (Barnes, Robertson); among them were not many wise (learned), mighty (powerful), or noble (high-born), the church largely composed of the humble and lowly (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin and Henry note God deliberately passed by the great of the world, choosing the lowly, that salvation might be manifestly of grace. The verse displays the lowly composition of the called. The verse marks the appeal: ye see your calling. The verse displays the composition: not many wise or noble. The verse displays the design: choosing the lowly. The verse displays the grace: not human advantage. The composition displays God's choice of the lowly. The verse displays the calling: of the humble. The verse displays the few: wise and noble. The verse displays the design: grace magnified. That not many wise, mighty, or noble are called displays God's deliberate choice of the lowly: the Corinthian church, composed largely of the humble and obscure, was itself proof that God passes by the great of the world, that salvation might manifestly be of grace and not of human advantage. The verse displays the lowly material of the church, and unfolds the divine purpose in choosing it (vv.27-29).
27:
"But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise; and God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty;" The witnesses note God choosing the foolish and weak to confound the wise and mighty. On "God hath chosen the foolish things... to confound the wise," the grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note God deliberately chose (selected) the things the world counts foolish (the lowly, the unlearned) precisely to confound (put to shame, humble) the wise.

On "the weak things... to confound the mighty," the witnesses note the parallel. Barnes notes God likewise chose the weak (the powerless) to confound the mighty — overthrowing human strength by means it despised.

The Reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the purpose: God's choice of the lowly is deliberate, designed to abase human pride and to display that the power and glory are wholly his. The verse displays God choosing the foolish and weak to confound the wise and mighty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God deliberately chose the things the world counts foolish (the lowly, unlearned) to confound (put to shame) the wise, and the weak (the powerless) to confound the mighty (Barnes, Robertson) — overthrowing human wisdom and strength by means they despised. Calvin notes the choice is deliberate, designed to abase human pride and display that the glory is wholly God's. The verse displays God's choice. The verse marks the foolish: to confound the wise. The verse displays the weak: to confound the mighty. The verse displays the design: deliberate choice. The verse displays the purpose: abasing pride. The choice displays God confounding the great by the lowly. The verse displays the foolish: chosen. The verse displays the weak: chosen. The verse displays the confounding: of the wise and mighty. God choosing the foolish and weak to confound the wise and mighty displays the deliberate divine strategy: by selecting the lowly and powerless, God puts to shame the wise and strong of the world, overthrowing human pride by the very means it despises. The verse displays God's purpose to abase the great by the lowly, and extends it to the things that are nought (v.28).
28:
"And base things of the world, and things which are despised, hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not, to bring to nought things that are:" The witnesses note God choosing the base and despised to nullify the things that are. On "base things... things which are despised," the grammatical voices note the further choice. Barnes and Robertson note God chose the base (low-born, ignoble) and despised (held in contempt) things of the world — the very objects of the world's scorn.

On "things which are not, to bring to nought things that are," the witnesses note the strongest contrast. Barnes and Calvin note God chose "things which are not" (treated as nothing, of no account) to bring to nought (annul, reduce to nothing) "things that are" (the esteemed, the established) — the lowliest overthrowing the greatest.

The Reformed Calvin notes the design: God exalts the nothings and abases the somethings, that all human glory may be levelled and his grace alone exalted. The verse displays God choosing the base and despised to nullify the things that are.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God chose the base (low-born, ignoble) and despised (held in contempt) things of the world — the objects of the world's scorn (Barnes, Robertson) — and even "things which are not" (treated as nothing) to bring to nought "things that are" (the esteemed and established), the lowliest overthrowing the greatest (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes God exalts the nothings and abases the somethings, levelling all human glory. The verse displays God's choice. The verse marks the base: chosen. The verse displays the despised: chosen. The verse displays the contrast: things that are not vs things that are. The verse displays the design: levelling human glory. The choice displays God nullifying the great by the nothing. The verse displays the base and despised: chosen. The verse displays the nothings: exalted. The verse displays the somethings: brought to nought. God choosing the base and despised to nullify the things that are displays the extremity of the divine strategy: he selects the world's nothings — the base, the scorned, the things counted as nothing — to bring to nought the things that are esteemed, levelling all human glory before his grace. The verse displays the radical reversal God works in the gospel, and states its purpose (v.29).
29:
"That no flesh should glory in his presence." The brief verse notes the purpose: that no flesh should glory before God. The grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note the grand purpose of God's choosing the lowly: that no flesh (no human being) should glory (boast) in his presence — all ground for human boasting removed.

On "in his presence," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes notes that before God, in the light of his presence, no one may claim merit or glory; salvation being wholly of grace, boasting is excluded.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: God so orders salvation that all human pride is excluded and the glory is his alone — the great end of the gospel's method. The verse displays the purpose: that no flesh should glory before God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the grand purpose of God's choosing the lowly is that no flesh (no human being) should glory (boast) in his presence — all ground for human boasting removed (Barnes, Robertson); before God no one may claim merit or glory, salvation being wholly of grace (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note God so orders salvation that all human pride is excluded and the glory is his alone. The verse displays the purpose. The verse marks the design: no flesh should glory. The verse displays the scope: in his presence. The verse displays the exclusion: of boasting. The verse displays the end: God's glory alone. The purpose displays the exclusion of all human boasting. The verse displays the flesh: silenced. The verse displays the presence: of God. The verse displays the glory: his alone. The purpose that no flesh should glory before God displays the grand end of his method: by choosing the lowly and confounding the great, God removes every ground of human boasting, so that in his presence no one may claim merit, and all the glory of salvation belongs to him alone. The verse displays the exclusion of pride as God's design, and locates the believer's true ground of glorying in Christ (vv.30-31).
30:
"But of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption:" The witnesses note Christ made unto us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption — and the imputation debate. On "of him are ye in Christ Jesus," the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note the believers' whole spiritual being is from God ("of him") — by his grace, not their own merit or philosophy, they are in Christ.

On the four benefits, the witnesses note the fulness. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson note Christ is made to us wisdom (true knowledge of God), righteousness (our justification), sanctification (our holiness), and redemption (our deliverance) — the whole of salvation found in him.

On "righteousness," the debate divides. The Reformed Calvin and Gill read it as imputation — Christ's obedience imputed to us for righteousness, the ground of our justification. The grammatical-historical Barnes denies the text proves imputation — it affirms we become righteous through Christ without specifying the mode, just as we become wise through him (not by imputed wisdom). The verse displays Christ made unto us all things, with the imputation crux.

The witnesses agree the believers' whole spiritual being is from God ("of him"), in Christ by grace not merit (Barnes, Robertson); and Christ is made to them wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption — the whole of salvation found in him (Calvin, Robertson). But "righteousness" divides them: Calvin and Gill read it as imputation (Christ's obedience imputed for righteousness, the ground of justification); Barnes denies the text proves imputation, holding it affirms we become righteous through Christ without specifying the mode, as we become wise through him (not by imputed wisdom). The verse displays Christ made unto us all things. The verse marks the source: of him, in Christ. The verse displays the benefits: wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption. The verse displays the debate: imputation or not. The verse displays the fulness: all in Christ. Christ made unto us all things displays the completeness of salvation in him. The verse displays the wisdom: true knowledge. The verse displays the righteousness: the crux. The verse displays the sanctification and redemption. Christ made unto us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption displays the fulness of salvation found in him alone: of God, the believers are in Christ, who is the whole of their wisdom, justification, holiness, and deliverance. The verse opens the imputation debate — whether "righteousness" here teaches Christ's imputed obedience (Calvin, Gill) or only that we become righteous through Christ without defining the mode (Barnes) — and grounds all glorying in the Lord (v.31).
31:
"That, according as it is written, He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord." The witnesses note the conclusion: let him that glorieth glory in the Lord. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes (the sense of) Jeremiah 9:24: he that glories must glory in the Lord — not in himself, his wisdom, or his works, but in God alone.

On "glory in the Lord," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Calvin note this is the grand conclusion of the whole passage: since all is of God and in Christ (v.30), the only legitimate boast is in the Lord — all self-glorying excluded.

The Reformed Calvin notes the coherence: because God has so arranged salvation that no flesh may glory (v.29) and Christ is made our all (v.30), it follows that our only glorying is in the Lord. The verse displays the conclusion: let him that glorieth glory in the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes the sense of Jeremiah 9:24 — he that glories must glory in the Lord, not in himself, his wisdom, or his works, but in God alone (Barnes, Robertson); this the grand conclusion of the whole passage, since all is of God and in Christ, the only legitimate boast being in the Lord (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the coherence: because no flesh may glory and Christ is made our all, our only glorying is in the Lord. The verse displays the conclusion. The verse marks the citation: Jeremiah. The verse displays the principle: glory in the Lord. The verse displays the exclusion: not in self. The verse displays the coherence: with vv.29-30. The conclusion displays the believer's only glorying in the Lord. The verse displays the boast: in the Lord. The verse displays the source: Jeremiah. The verse displays the exclusion: of self. The conclusion — let him that glorieth glory in the Lord — displays the believer's only legitimate boast: since all wisdom, righteousness, holiness, and redemption are of God and in Christ, and no flesh may glory in his presence, the saved man boasts not in himself but wholly in the Lord. The verse displays the great principle that crowns the passage on the cross and human wisdom, and leads into Paul's own example of preaching (ch. 2).
